title: Stephen Foster and That Old, Weird America
gist: Ed Simon reads Stephen Foster — born the same day Jefferson and Adams died — as the true prototype of American popular culture: a Westerner and proto-pop-star whose enduring folk melodies (Oh Susanna, Beautiful Dreamer) built the country's first mass-media celebrity, even as his commercially dominant blackface minstrel songs (My Old Kentucky Home, Old Black Joe) embody Eric Lott's 'love and theft' thesis of predatory racial appropriation, a legacy still being publicly reckoned with (a 2018 statue removal) despite Frederick Douglass's own ambivalent embrace of Foster's tunes as antislavery 'heart songs.'

insights.claim: Stephen Foster was born in Pittsburgh on July 4, 1826, the same day Thomas Jefferson and John Adams both died — the fiftieth anniversary of the Declaration of Independence — a coincidence President John Quincy Adams called 'visible and palpable remarks of Divine Favor' and which later scholars used to burnish a hagiography of Foster as representative of American democracy.
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: Establishes the article's core structural device: reading Foster's cultural significance through the symbolic coincidence of his birth against the Founders' deaths.
insights.quote: visible and palpable remarks of Divine Favor
insights.locator: The then-occupant of the White House

themes.theme: Foster as the first American pop star

insights.claim: Simon argues Foster — not Jefferson or Adams — represents the true emergence of the American type: not a Northern revolutionary elitist or a Southern slave-owning Republican but a Westerner, signaling a shift from the Founders' European Enlightenment experiment to 'a nation of self-mythologizing grifters and confidence men, of boot-strappers and self-inventors.'
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: Reframes American national identity formation as beginning with a commercial pop-culture figure rather than a political one — a lens for reading American mass culture as constitutive of national identity from its earliest moments.
insights.quote: a nation of self-mythologizing grifters and confidence men, of boot-strappers and self-inventors.
insights.locator: The Founders conceived of a particular nation

insights.claim: Foster became the best-selling songwriter on both sides of the Mason-Dixon Line and the Mississippi River purely through sheet-music sales, decades before Thomas Edison's 1877 invention of the wax cylinder made recorded sound possible.
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: Establishes that mass American celebrity and cross-regional cultural reach predate recorded media by decades, driven entirely by print/sheet-music distribution — relevant to understanding pre-recording-era media economics.
insights.quote: the free-spending Foster, through the selling of sheet music alone, became the most popular songwriter on both sides of the Mason-Dixon Line and the Mississippi River.
insights.locator: Dead almost three decades before Thomas Edison

insights.claim: Bob Dylan has repeatedly cited Foster as a foundational influence, dedicating a chapter of The Philosophy of Modern Song to Foster's 1849 song 'Nelly Was a Lady' and calling it, in his words, one of the saddest songs ever written.
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: Documents a direct lineage of influence from Foster to one of the most commercially and critically significant songwriters of the modern era, reinforcing Foster's outsized and durable cultural footprint.
insights.quote: A lot of sad songs have been written,” Dylan writes, “but none sadder than this.
insights.locator: I go back to Stephen Foster

themes.theme: The minstrel legacy and its racial politics

insights.claim: Foster's commercial fame rested specifically on his blackface minstrel songs (which he personally considered 'trashy and vulgar' and preferred his European-style parlor tunes), which literary scholar Eric Lott's 'love and theft' thesis frames as simultaneously predatory appropriation of blackness and a structurally white-supremacist performance mode, 'underwritten by envy as well as repulsion, sympathetic identification as well as fear.'
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: Provides the article's central interpretive framework for evaluating Foster's legacy — commercial success built on a genre the artist himself disdained but that carried a specific ideological function (Lott's 'love and theft').
insights.quote: Underwritten by envy as well as repulsion, sympathetic identification as well as fear

insights.claim: Despite writing minstrel-dialect lyrics for songs like 'My Old Kentucky Home' (now Kentucky's official state song) and 'Old Black Joe,' Foster's actual exposure to Black communities was almost entirely theoretical — Pittsburgh's Black population was only about 3% at his birth — and his only direct exposure to the South was a single honeymoon riverboat trip down the Mississippi.
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: Undercuts any claim of authenticity behind Foster's minstrel material — the commercially dominant genre of his catalog was built on imagined rather than observed experience, reinforcing the 'appropriation' reading of his work.
insights.quote: blacks made up only about three percent of Pittsburgh’s population
insights.locator: Pennsylvania's first abolition act was passed in 1780

insights.claim: Abolitionist Frederick Douglass paradoxically praised Foster's minstrel tunes as 'heart songs' in an 1855 essay, claiming they awaken 'sympathies for the slave, in which anti-slavery principles take root, grow, and flourish' — a strategic endorsement from the era's leading Black abolitionist that complicates a simple condemnation of Foster's legacy.
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: Introduces a genuine historical counterweight to the appropriation critique — a contemporary Black abolitionist leader saw at least strategic anti-slavery value in Foster's songs, complicating retrospective judgment.
insights.quote: sympathies for the slave, in which anti-slavery principles take root, grow, and flourish.
insights.locator: The orator and abolitionist Frederick Douglass

insights.claim: A 1900 Pittsburgh statue by Giuseppe Moretti depicting a barefoot enslaved man ('Old Ned') playing banjo at Foster's feet was removed in 2018 after a unanimous vote by the city's Art Commission, evidencing an ongoing, unresolved public reckoning with Foster's minstrel-era imagery.
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated data point (2018 removal) marking where public institutions have already acted to reassess Foster's legacy — relevant to tracking further reassessment of Confederate/minstrel-adjacent public monuments.
insights.quote: unveiled in 1900 and (appropriately) removed in 2018 after the unanimous decision of Pittsburgh’s Art Commission
insights.locator: across the street there used to sit a statue

insights.claim: Foster's father William Barclay Foster, a three-time Pennsylvania state legislator and former mayor of Allegheny, built a handsome family manor in 1826 that the bank repossessed a decade later — undercutting the hagiographic myth that Foster came from nowhere, and showing that financial precarity was a family trait Foster himself would later repeat.
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: Complicates the 'self-made American' myth built around Foster by showing his family's financial instability predated and likely shaped his own later insolvency.
insights.quote: the bank would repossess the handsome home that Foster’s father built
insights.locator: A decade after the white-cottage-with-green-trim-windows was built

insights.claim: Foster died broke in New York's Bellevue Hospital in 1864, possibly suffering alcohol withdrawal, with only 37 cents in his pocket and an unfinished lyric fragment beginning 'Dear friends and gentle hearts' — dying the same year as the Civil War's Battle of the Wilderness and March to the Sea.
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: A stark biographical data point on the gap between Foster's outsized cultural/commercial impact and his personal financial failure — relevant to any narrative about the economics of authorship in the pre-copyright-enforcement, sheet-music era.
insights.quote: He had only 37 cents in his pocket (one less than his age) and an envelope on which he had scratched some half-formed lyrics.
insights.locator: Living beyond one's means was a family trait

insights.claim: Foster's melodies have been repeatedly quoted or reinterpreted across two centuries by classical composers Aaron Copland, Charles Ives, and Steve Reich, and covered by performers from Willie Nelson and Yo-Yo Ma to Bruce Springsteen, Emmylou Harris, and Mavis Staples, evidence his musical craft outlived and partly transcended the racist lyrical content that made him famous.
insights.speaker: Ed Simon
insights.so_what: Documents durable, decades-long cross-genre licensing/covering value in Foster's melodic catalog independent of the original lyrics — relevant to understanding how contested-legacy cultural IP can retain commercial and artistic value via reinterpretation.
insights.quote: would be quoted as musical motifs by Aaron Copland, Charles Ives, and Steve Reich. And long after his death, his songs would be covered by everyone from Willie Nelson to Yo-Yo Ma.
insights.locator: In the twentieth century, his melodies
